← the Atlas

Canvas tiles that show their state — border prototypes

feature · seedling ·implemented ·JUN 13, 2026

Five terminals on the canvas; which one needs you? Surface each session's run-state on the tile border itself — a loudness ladder ranked by attention (alert ▸ fresh-waiting ▸ working ▸ stale-waiting ▸ idle), where "waiting" cools with last-activity age via kolu's existing activity-window. Four live, animated visual languages to choose from.

Contents

  • The attention ladder
  • Waiting cools as it ages
  • The active tile — where focus meets the ladder
  • Prototypes
    • A — Halo (recommended)
    • B — Live border
    • C — Sweep
    • D — Top bar (reads at any zoom)
  • Open decisions
    • Working color — rust, not teal
    • Motion = needs-you (working stays steady)
    • The decay curve & threshold
    • The active tile’s aura — mute or suppress
    • Idle / done, settings, reduced motion
  • How it wires in

With five terminals open on the canvas it’s hard to tell at a glance which one is thinking, which is waiting for you, and which just pinged you and you missed it. That state already exists — it shows as a label in each tile’s title bar (Thinking, Waiting for input, Running in background) and as colored dots in the dock. But reading a title bar isn’t a glance, and you’re looking at the terminals, not the dock. So put the signal on the tile border, where peripheral vision can catch it across the whole canvas.

The ask

Make running terminals visually distinct on the canvas. An animating border for ones that are working, a throbbing border for ones with a fresh alert, and some other border for ones that are waiting on you — so a sweep of the eye tells you the state of the fleet without reading anything.

This note is a chooser: four animated border languages rendered live below, each across the full attention ladder — which is ranked (some tiles matter more than others) and time-aware (a “waiting” tile cools as it ages) — plus the secondary decisions (color, motion, decay, settings). Pick a language and the open decisions, and it ships on the throbborder branch.

The attention ladder

The signal isn’t a handful of flat colors — it’s a loudness ladder ordered by who needs you: alert ▸ fresh-waiting ▸ working ▸ stale-waiting ▸ idle. And one rung isn’t fixed — “waiting” slides down the ladder as it ages. A tile that started waiting a minute ago should outrank a busy one; the same tile six hours later has cooled below it. kolu already ranks and ages exactly this, so the border reuses the machinery rather than inventing a parallel scheme:

RankStateWhenColorBorder
1 · Alertunread — flipped to needing you while you weren’t lookingviolet --color-alertfast throb + halo (~1.2s); self-clears on focus
2 · Waiting · freshawaiting and recent — within your activity window (esp. < 4h)violet --color-alertgentle slow breathe, bright
3 · Workingthinking / running tools / background workflowrust --color-busysteady hum — no motion
4 · Waiting · staleawaiting but older than your activity window (parked)violet --color-alertdim ember, static — cooled below the working hum
5 · Idle / doneno agent, finished, or acknowledgedrepo identitynone

Two refinements the priority order forces

1 · “Waiting” splits in two and decays. A fresh waiter outranks a working tile; the same tile, hours later, has cooled below it. The crossover is your activity window — the 4h / 12h / 24h / All control already in the sidebar (packages/client/src/terminal/activityWindow.ts, default 24h). Pick 4h and a waiter drops below the working hum after four hours; All disables decay so every waiter stays fresh. The loudest rung (alert) still clears itself the instant you focus the tile.

2 · Motion means “needs you,” not “busy.” Because alert (1) and fresh-waiting (2) outrank working (3), the strongest attention cue — motion — is reserved for the top of the ladder. Working is a steady glow; only the rungs that want you actually move. This flips the first instinct (an animating border for working); see Open decisions below.

Waiting cools as it ages

This is the new axis. The same waiting tile, rendered at four ages — its violet glow dims monotonically and stops moving as lastActivityAt recedes, crossing below the rust working hum at your activity-window threshold and settling near idle once it parks. The clock is kolu’s existing useStaleCheck() / activityWindow — the very signal that already parks dock rows and fades minimap tiles, now cooling the canvas border too. One vocabulary, one persisted choice.

Waiting · cooling with last-activity age — your activity window is the crossover
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting · just now
just now
rank 2 — loud
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting · ~2h
~2h
still above the hum
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting · ~4h
~4h
crossing your window
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting · stale · parked
stale · parked
rank 4 — ember

The first tile is the live rank-2 breathe; the rest are the same state cooling. The crossover (where waiting dips under working) lands wherever you set the activity window — so the decay is yours to tune, not a hard-coded curve.

The active tile — where focus meets the ladder

The ladder ranks attention demand — which tile to look at next. Focus marks where you are. Different axes, and the active tile is where they meet, so make the composition explicit:

  • Focus is its own channel, already built. The active tile carries a teal inner ring + a right-edge accent (store.activeId() → the existing 0 0 0 1px var(--color-accent)). Teal = “you are here”; the rust/violet aura = “this wants you.” Two colors, two geometries — inner ring vs outer glow — so they don’t collide, and rust-for-working keeps the hues apart.
  • The active tile is never alert. Focusing a tile clears its unread (existing behavior — sound, OS badge, and pip all reset). So the loudest rung only ever lights an inactive tile: the eye-grab always points you somewhere new, never back at yourself.
  • Focus mutes the tile’s own aura. You’re already attending to it, so it shouldn’t compete — the --aura-intensity knob simply scales down on data-active. An active working tile is a clean teal ring with its rust aura dialed to a whisper; an active waiting tile keeps a faint violet (you can still see it wants input) while focus dominates.
One active tile (teal · "you are here") among the ladder — the loudest border is never the active one
argh · claude
◐ Thinking…
Active · working — focus wins, aura muted
kaval · codex
⏵ Awaiting input
Alert — loudest, always inactive
blog · claude
⏵ Waiting · fresh
Waiting · fresh — inactive
odu · claude
◐ Running tools
Working — inactive hum

One compositional rule. A tile’s effective border loudness is state-tier × age-decay × presence, where presence is the existing opacity hierarchy (active 1.0 · inactive 0.92 · parked 0.55) — a box-shadow aura fades with its element’s opacity for free. Active additionally mutes the aura; parked is already dim. The product guarantees the brightest border on the canvas is always an inactive, fresh, attention-class tile — precisely where the eye should land. Focus and the ladder reinforce each other instead of fighting.

Prototypes

Four border languages, each rendered live across the ladder (waiting shown fresh). They differ in which channel carries state and how energetic it reads — and two tiebreakers now matter: which can express a continuous loudness ramp (so a waiter can dim), and which survives scaling to the minimap so the canvas and its map speak one language.

A — Halo (recommended)

Recommended A soft outer glow carries state; the border line stays the repo identity color. A glow has a continuous brightness axis, so it’s the only language that renders the whole ranked, aging ladder cleanly — alert throbs brightest, fresh-waiting breathes a bright violet, working sits as a steady rust hum, and a stale waiter just dims toward an ember (the decay strip above is literally this language turned down). It also leaves the two existing border meanings — repo identity and the focus ring — untouched.

A · Halo — glow (idle ▸ working ▸ waiting·fresh ▸ alert)
argh · claude
$ ▏
Idle / done
argh · claude
◐ Thinking…
Working
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting for input
Waiting · fresh
argh · claude
⏵ Awaiting input
Alert (unread)

B — Live border

The border line itself takes the state color — the most literal reading of “animated border.” It can honor the rank (steady rust for working, a breathing violet for fresh-waiting, a throb for alert), but two problems: it overwrites the repo-identity color while an agent is busy (most of the time), and a solid line has nowhere to dim to — a faint border reads as a rendering bug, not a cooled waiter, so the decay ramp has no room. Fine for a binary; awkward for a ranked, aging ladder.

B · Live border — the line carries state
argh · claude
$ ▏
Idle / done
argh · claude
◐ Thinking…
Working
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting for input
Waiting · fresh
argh · claude
⏵ Awaiting input
Alert (unread)

C — Sweep

A comet of light marches around the border while working — the most “this is actively running right now” of the set, echoing the dock rail’s orbiting glow. But that’s the trap under the priority order: it spends the loudest channel (continuous motion) on the lowest-priority active state — working, rank 3 — pulling the eye to a churning tile over one that’s actually blocked on you. The rank inverts it. Keep it only if “what’s running” matters more than “what’s waiting.” (Attention states fall back to the halo throb.)

C · Sweep — a marching comet for working
argh · claude
$ ▏
Idle / done
argh · claude
◐ Thinking…
Working
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting for input
Waiting · fresh
argh · claude
⏵ Awaiting input
Alert (unread)

D — Top bar (reads at any zoom)

A thin status bar on the top edge — steady rust for working, a gentle violet pulse for fresh-waiting, a sharper blink for alert, nothing for idle. It rings nothing and stays out of the title bar, so it’s the calmest of the four. Its real edge is scale-invariance: a colored top edge is the same legible shape on a full canvas tile or a 40px minimap marker — which the others can’t claim (a glow or a marching comet dissolves when the tile is tiny).

D · Top bar — top-edge status strip
argh · claude
$ ▏
Idle / done
argh · claude
◐ Thinking…
Working
argh · claude
⏵ Waiting for input
Waiting · fresh
argh · claude
⏵ Awaiting input
Alert (unread)

And the minimap already wants a state signal: today it paints a corner dot (bucketDescriptor().accentVar). Language D unifies the two surfaces — the same top edge, scaled down — so the canvas and its map speak one language:

D in the minimap — the same top-edge bar, scaled down (replaces today's corner dot)
teal ring = active · rust = working · violet pulse = waiting · dim/short violet = stale · blink = alert

Decay survives here too — dim the bar’s opacity and shrink its width as a waiter ages (the short, faint violet marker above is a stale one): a draining gauge instead of a fading glow. The honest cost vs Halo is expressive range — a 2.5px bar carries less nuance than a continuous glow, and a top edge is easier to miss peripherally / when a tile is overlapped. You trade richness for one-shape-everywhere coherence.

Open decisions

Independent of the language, a few choices shape how loud and how broad this gets.

Working color — rust, not teal

The active/focused tile already owns teal (--color-accent: the focus ring + the right-edge accent). If “working” is also teal, a focused-and-working tile is teal-on-teal — is it busy, or is it the one I’m looking at? Coloring working rust (--color-busy) keeps the two apart, and it matches the rust “Thinking” icon already sitting in that tile’s title bar. Three legible hues: teal = you’re here · rust = it’s busy · violet = it wants you.

Focused + working — teal ring (focus) vs the working glow
argh · claude
◐ Thinking…
Rust glow — clearly distinct
argh · claude
◐ Thinking…
Teal glow — reads ambiguous

Note: the dock uses teal for working

The dock pip colors working teal today, so rust-on-canvas is a deliberate divergence (it dodges the focus collision the dock never has). Worth a follow-up to reconcile — either align the dock to rust, or accept the two surfaces reading working differently because only the canvas has a teal focus ring competing.

Motion = needs-you (working stays steady)

The priority order mostly answers the earlier “should working animate?” question: no — if a busy tile out-pulses a blocked one, the eye lands in the wrong place. So motion is reserved for the rungs that want you (alert throbs, fresh-waiting breathes) and working is a steady hum (as shown). The remaining lever is taste: fully static working, or a barely-there breath so it still reads “alive” — as long as it stays clearly quieter than fresh-waiting.

The decay curve & threshold

How “waiting” cools is its own decision:

  • Continuous vs banded. A smooth --aura-intensity from age / threshold (the strip above is hand-stepped to fake it), or reuse the existing age bands — idleBucketFor() already cuts 4h-12h / 12h-24h / 24h-48h / 48h+ (packages/client/src/terminal/activityWindow.ts) for discrete steps with zero new math.
  • Which window. Reuse the persisted, user-chosen activityWindow (default 24h); honor All = no decay. The one control already in the sidebar drives dock, minimap, and this.
  • Where stale lands. A dim violet ember (still legibly “waiting”), or fully demote to idle/parked to match how the dock and minimap already treat parked rows. Ember keeps the “it wanted you” memory; parked is quieter.

The active tile’s aura — mute or suppress

The active tile already wins via the teal ring and can’t be alert (focus clears unread). The open call is the rest of the ladder on it: mute to a whisper (~25–35% intensity, so you still glimpse working/waiting on the tile you’re in), or fully suppress (focus ring only, zero aura — calmest, leans hardest on “borders are for the tiles you’re not in”). Default: mute.

Idle / done, settings, reduced motion

  • Idle / done stays bare (repo border only) — don’t add noise to the quiet ones. A separate “just finished” pulse is possible but is arguably the alert rung’s job already.
  • Reduced motion follows the existing convention — every gallery above collapses to a static high-opacity state under prefers-reduced-motion: reduce; nothing depends on motion alone to be readable.
  • Settings — likely on by default (reduced-motion covers the calm case); a PreferencesSchema toggle is a small add if an explicit off-switch is wanted.

How it wires in

Small and contained — no new state machinery, because the upstream classifiers already exist and are the single source of truth. What actually shipped (Language D, the top bar):

  • A pure tier mapper, not a generalized dock mapper. Rather than fold the decision into the dock’s packages/client/src/canvas/dock/pipVariant.ts, the canvas got its own pure classifier packages/client/src/canvas/tileAura.ts: tileAura(bucket, unread, stale) → TileAura (alert | waiting | waiting-stale | working | none). It reuses the same upstream inputs the dock reads (agentBucket, the unread flag, useStaleCheck) but maps to the canvas’s own vocabulary — a top bar with a fresh/stale split on waiting. Kept a pure function so it’s unit-tested without a Solid harness (packages/client/src/canvas/tileAura.test.ts), the way pipVariant is.
  • Stale folds into the tier — consistently. A stale waiting tile cools to the static waiting-stale ember; a stale working tile is parked and drops to none (no bar), so the canvas agrees with the dock’s parked bucket (packages/client/src/canvas/dock/dockRowRanking.ts) and the minimap’s parked ghost rather than advertising a bright working hum on a tile the dock has already quieted.
  • One reactive socket, two surfaces. packages/client/src/canvas/useTileAura.ts closes over the terminal store and one useStaleCheck() instance and turns an id into its tier; the canvas tile (via packages/client/src/canvas/TerminalCanvas.tsx) and the minimap marker (packages/client/src/canvas/CanvasMinimap.tsx) both read it, so they can’t drift. Age comes from the same reactive, 60s-ticking staleness the dock and minimap already use (packages/client/src/terminal/staleness.ts, packages/client/src/terminal/activityWindow.ts) — no new threshold, no new timer.
  • The tile renders the bar. packages/client/src/canvas/CanvasTile.tsx takes the tier as a prop, sets data-aura on its root, and paints an absolutely-positioned top-edge .aura-bar (skipped when maximized or none). Colour + motion live entirely in CSS keyed off data-aura — there is no --aura-intensity custom property and no box-shadow glow; the active tile is muted by a fixed opacity: 0.35 on [data-active="true"] .aura-bar, not a scaled intensity.
  • CSS. The .aura-bar rules, aura-pulse / aura-blink keyframes, and the prefers-reduced-motion fallback live in packages/client/src/index.css beside the existing pill-* / dock-chip-* animations. The minimap reuses them at a thinner scale via .aura-bar--mini.

No new state or timing machinery — a CSS block, a small pure mapper plus its reactive socket, and the prop wiring on the tile and minimap. (No tip was added; the bar is self-evident enough that discoverability didn’t warrant a tips-registry entry.)

Status: implemented in #1335 — Language D (top bar), fresh-vs-stale decay (reusing useStaleCheck), and a muted active-tile bar. Shipped on throbborder.
